critique.summary: Physicists at Brown University studied the fluid dynamics of thin liquid films in kitchen containers, using the Navier-Stokes equations and experiments to determine how long it takes to drain the last drops of liquids like milk and oil. Their findings show that the drainage time varies significantly with viscosity, with low-viscosity liquids draining in seconds and high-viscosity liquids taking minutes or hours. The research also explored practical applications such as optimizing drying time for a cast iron wok to prevent rust while preserving seasoning.
mainPoints: Researchers at Brown University used fluid mechanics and experiments to study how long it takes to drain the last drops of liquid from tilted kitchen containers.
The drainage time depends on the liquid's viscosity, with milk draining 90% in about 30 seconds and olive oil taking over nine minutes.
Water drains very quickly, while viscous liquids like cold maple syrup can take hours to drain 90%.
The study applied these principles to a practical problem of drying a cast iron wok without damaging its seasoning, finding an optimal wait time of about 15 minutes before dumping residual water.
The research serves both as a training exercise in fluid mechanics and as a way to connect everyday experiences with scientific principles.
critique.truthfulness: The article accurately describes the application of the Navier-Stokes equations to model fluid flow and thin film drainage, which is a well-established area of fluid dynamics. The reported times for drainage of liquids with different viscosities are plausible and consistent with known physical principles. The involvement of Brown University physicists and the mention of publication in the journal Physics of Fluids add credibility. The practical wok drying advice is based on simulations and aligns with common knowledge about seasoning preservation and rust prevention. However, the article relies primarily on the researchers' own study without citing independent sources or peer commentary, which is typical for university press releases but limits external verification.
considerations: The article does not provide direct citations or links to the original published study for independent verification.
It does not include perspectives or validation from other experts in fluid dynamics or kitchen science.
The practical recommendations, such as the 15-minute wait time for drying a wok, may vary depending on environmental conditions like humidity and temperature.
The study focuses on idealized conditions (e.g., a plate tilted at 45 degrees) which may differ from real-world container shapes and angles.
The article does not discuss potential variability in liquid properties (e.g., temperature effects on viscosity) that could affect drainage times.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the accuracy of the article's claims about the time to drain liquids from kitchen containers. One commenter points out the article only addresses the time to remove most liquid, not the last drop, while another shares related research experience.

comments.author: distances
comments.text: Interesting, but the headline was misleading -- this was about the time to get the first 90%. The last drop was not discussed. I was already intrigued about how one could know when a drop was the last drop.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately identifies a key limitation in the article's scope, clarifying that the last drop's removal time was not addressed.

comments.author: sudhirb
comments.text: I can hardly believe my eyes! I helped do some related research specifically concerning thin-film drainage from tubes, way back in my undergraduate days: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.expthermflusci.2018.04.015
comments.analysis: The comment provides credible background and references related research, supporting the discussion with relevant scientific work.

comments.author: jiehong
comments.text:
Interesting!

For the wok example, I recommend just putting the wok back on the stove for a few seconds and have the thin water film evaporated, before quickly coating it with an oiled tissue, and store it hot. It takes 30 seconds max.
comments.analysis: This practical advice is plausible but anecdotal and does not directly address the scientific aspects of liquid drainage timing.
